title: TabICL: A Tabular Foundation Model for In-Context Learning on Large Data
authors: Jingang Qu, David Holzmüller, Gaël Varoquaux, Marine Le Morvan (SODA team, INRIA Saclay; Sierra team, INRIA Paris / ENS PSL)
tokenization_method: Two-stage 'column-then-row' tokenization that collapses the column dimension into a single fixed-size row embedding before in-context learning, built from THREE stacked transformers. (1) Distribution-aware column-wise embedding (TFcol): each column c_j (a set of n scalar cell values) is fed to a SHARED Set Transformer (ISAB: induced self-attention with k=128 inducing vectors, 3 blocks, d=128) that acts as a HYPERNETWORK emitting per-cell weight/bias (W,B); the cell embedding is e = W ⊙ c + B. So one column-embedding module is shared across ALL features (no per-feature lookup table), yet adapts to each column's distribution. (2) Context-aware row-wise interaction (TFrow): a 3-layer, 8-head transformer runs across the m feature embeddings within each row; four learnable [CLS] tokens are prepended and their outputs concatenated to give a 4×d=512-dim row embedding H. RoPE (rotary positional embedding, scale base 100,000) is injected here to break symmetry between identically-distributed features and prevent representation collapse. (3) Dataset-wise ICL (TFicl): a 12-layer, 4-head transformer takes the row embeddings H plus one-hot training labels (added to train embeddings) and predicts test labels in one forward pass; test rows attend only to train rows. A 2-layer MLP head emits class probabilities.
feature_types_handled: numerical and categorical (mixed), classification targets only. Categoricals are ordinal-encoded into scalars and then z-normalized (with or without power transform) exactly like numericals — there is NO dedicated categorical token type; the shared distribution-aware column embedder is expected to recover categorical structure from the value distribution. Missing values in the eval pipeline are mean-imputed by the TALENT benchmark (not handled in-model in the reported experiments). No native temporal/time axis.
token_granularity: per-cell at the embedding stage (one d-dim vector per (sample, feature) cell, produced by TFcol), then aggregated to a per-ROW token: after row-wise interaction each row is compressed to a single 512-dim embedding via 4 [CLS] tokens. The ICL transformer therefore operates on one token per row, NOT one token per cell — this column-collapse is the key efficiency move vs TabPFNv2's persistent per-cell grid.
numerical_strategy: scalar cells z-normalized (optionally power-transformed) up front, then mapped through the shared Set-Transformer hypernetwork that outputs a distribution-aware affine embedding e = W⊙c + B. The embedding is distribution-aware (PCA of the learned per-column representation clusters columns by skewness/kurtosis), so it captures a value's statistical role (min/max/mean/mode) rather than using a fixed piecewise-linear or periodic basis like PLR. Not a hand-designed numerical basis — it is learned and shared.
categorical_strategy: ordinal encoding to scalars, then the SAME shared distribution-aware column embedder used for numericals (no separate per-category lookup, no learned category tokens, no target encoding). Categorical-ness is implicit, recovered from the discrete value distribution by TFcol.
high_cardinality_handling: no dedicated mechanism. Because there is no per-feature/per-category embedding table, parameter count is independent of cardinality; but high-cardinality categoricals are just discrete scalars fed through the shared column embedder, so very-high-cardinality fields are not specially modeled. Authors report TabICL performs well on high ratios of categorical features (Appendix B) but do not isolate extreme-cardinality behavior.
temporal_handling: none. Each row is a static unordered feature vector; no patching, recurrence, or channel-as-token. Time-varying features must be hand-flattened to static columns before use.
scales_to_many_features: Pretrained on synthetic datasets with up to 60K samples and ≤100 features (≤10 classes); at inference handles up to ~500K samples and ~500 features. Complexity is O(m^2·n + n^2): TFcol is O(n·k·m) via ISAB (linear in n), TFrow is O(m^2·n) (cheap since m≪n), and the quadratic-in-n ICL cost O(n^2) is paid ONCE on the column-collapsed per-row tokens. Contrast TabPFNv2 at O(m^2·n + n^2·m) — TabICL drops a factor of m on the dominant ICL term, the source of its 3-10x speedup on large data. Memory: 100K×500 in ~5GB GPU + 32GB RAM via FlashAttention + dynamic batch sizing + CPU/disk activation offload; 500K×500 in ~20GB GPU. RoPE base 100,000 is used to extrapolate to more features (up to 100 seen in training) than at train time.
downstream_model: the three-transformer stack IS the model — a single frozen pretrained PFN; no per-dataset head training. Train rows are in-context examples; test rows predicted in one forward pass.
pretraining_objective: in-context Bayesian-style classification (PFN posterior-predictive approximation). Pretrained EXCLUSIVELY on synthetic datasets from structural causal models (DAG-structured MLPs, c = f(Pa(c)) + noise). Two enrichments over TabPFN v1's prior: (i) 30% of datasets use TREE-BASED SCMs where f is an XGBoost regressor trained on fake Gaussian targets (injecting GBDT-style hierarchical/interaction inductive bias), 70% standard SCMs; (ii) ~15 extra activation functions plus Gaussian-Process-sampled random activations for richer non-linearities. Curriculum learning over dataset SIZE: stage 1 fixed 1,024 samples (160K steps), stage 2 log-uniform 1K-40K (2K steps), stage 3 uniform 40K-60K training ONLY TFicl with the rest frozen (50 steps). 20 days on 3×A100-40GB.
key_results: TALENT benchmark, 200 classification datasets (171 with ≤10 classes used as native regime). Best median relative accuracy over a tuned MLP across all datasets; on the critical-difference diagram TabICL and TabPFNv2 outperform 30+ baselines by a wide margin and are NOT statistically distinguishable from each other. Speed: geometric-mean train+infer ~1.1s per 1K samples vs ~3 min to tune CatBoost (CPU) and ~7 min for RealMLP/ModernNCA (GPU); 1.5x faster than TabPFNv2 on small data, 3-10x faster on large data (10K×100: ~20s vs ~1m40s). On the 53 datasets with >10K samples, TabICL SURPASSES both TabPFNv2 and CatBoost (TabPFNv2 must subsample to 30K and degrades; pretrained only to 2048 samples). Also best/near-best on log loss (reliable probabilities), and 2nd-best on the 12 >10-class datasets via hierarchical classification (TabPFNv2 cannot handle >10 classes natively).
baselines_compared: TabPFNv2, TabPFN(v1), CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, RandomForest, ModernNCA, RealMLP, TabR, TabM, FT-Transformer, ResNet, MLP/MLP-PLR, DCNv2, AutoInt, TuneTables, LoCalPFN, NODE, TabNet, SAINT-family, plus ~30 TALENT baselines. Compared on accuracy, log loss, and wall-clock time.
transferable_idea: Collapse the column dimension into ONE fixed-size per-row token via a SHARED distribution-aware Set-Transformer column embedder, so the expensive sample-axis ICL attention is paid once on row tokens (O(n^2)) instead of on the full cell grid (O(n^2·m)). For 70+ heterogeneous features this is the most reusable piece: (a) a single shared column-embedder (Set Transformer as hypernetwork emitting per-cell W,B) that ingests any number of features with NO per-feature parameters and adapts to each column's distribution — directly attacks parameter blow-up and cross-table transfer; (b) RoPE over the feature axis to de-collapse identically-distributed features without inventing random feature-id vectors; (c) the column-collapse-then-ICL split as a general recipe to make wide tables tractable.
limitations: Authors-stated: (1) classification ONLY (regression sketched but not implemented). (2) RoPE BREAKS column-permutation invariance — a defining tabular property — only approximately restored by ensembling over 32 column shuffles. (3) inference is slow vs GBDT (foundation-model forward pass); caching could help. (4) eval inherits TALENT's choices (mean imputation, single-model holdout tuning of baselines, no bagging). (5) representation collapse when many features share an identical distribution (the failure RoPE is patching). Implicit: pretrained only ≤100 features / ≤2048 samples per step, so the 500-feature / 500K-sample inference regime relies on extrapolation (RoPE base, curriculum) rather than in-distribution training; categoricals/high-cardinality and missing values have no dedicated in-model treatment in the reported setup.
pitfalls: 1) The headline '500K samples / 500 features' is an INFERENCE-extrapolation ceiling, NOT the pretraining regime (≤60K samples, ≤100 features, ≤10 classes per step). Wide-table (>100 feature) behavior is out-of-distribution and leans on RoPE extrapolation + ensembling — verify before trusting at the topic's 70+-feature scale, which sits right at/above the training feature ceiling. 2) Column-permutation invariance is intentionally sacrificed for RoPE; the '32-shuffle ensemble' that restores it is a 32x inference-cost tax that the 1.1s/1K-samples headline already bakes in but that complicates serving. 3) Categoricals are just ordinal-encoded scalars through the shared embedder — there is NO real categorical/high-cardinality tokenization, so do not cite TabICL as a categorical-encoding advance; its strength is numerical/distribution-aware embedding + scaling. 4) The >10K-sample win over CatBoost is partly because TabPFNv2 was forced to subsample to 30K; it is a scaling/efficiency story, and GBDTs may still win at industrial >>100K-row scale not covered. 5) 'Distribution-aware' embedding is emergent from the synthetic SCM prior, including a 30% tree-based-SCM component explicitly seeded to mimic GBDT bias — the inductive bias is borrowed from trees, not discovered independently.
relevance_to_topic: The cleanest demonstration that a SHARED, distribution-aware column embedder plus a column-collapse-to-row-token step lets one transformer ingest many heterogeneous features and scale to large n cheaply — directly relevant to tokenizing 70+ mixed features — but it offers no real categorical/high-cardinality or temporal tokenization and sits at the edge of its ≤100-feature training regime.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/soda-inria/tabicl (pretraining code, inference code, and pretrained weights released).
